original_abstract:
This study presents a comprehensive mechanistic investigation into the oxidative addition of aryl iodides to Ni(I) complexes, a fundamental but poorly understood step in many modern catalytic manifolds. Utilizing an integrated approach that combines electroanalytical techniques with statistical modeling, the authors circumvent the challenges associated with the instability and complex speciation of Ni(I) species. 

By employing cyclic voltammetry for the in situ generation of low-valent nickel complexes, the researchers measured over 200 oxidative addition rate constants across sixteen distinct catalyst systems—including bipyridine, phenanthroline, terpyridine, and bis-pyrazolylpyridine (BPP) ligands—and a diverse library of 44 aryl iodide substrates. Systematic analysis of electronic and steric effects through Linear Free-Energy Relationships (LFER) and multivariate linear regression (MLR) modeling revealed distinct mechanistic pathways: while complexes with bipyridine, phenanthroline, and terpyridine ligands typically proceed via a three-center concerted mechanism, the BPP-supported Ni(I) complexes appear to operate through a halogen-atom abstraction pathway. 

To demonstrate the utility of these insights, the researchers developed a global heat map of predicted oxidative addition rates for over 2,000 commercially available aryl iodides. Finally, a case study on a Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling reaction illustrates how these predicted kinetic trends can rationalize reaction yields and identify potential side-reaction pathways, offering a predictive platform for the optimization and design of future nickel-catalyzed transformations.
humanized_abstract:
Oxidative addition of aryl iodides to Ni(I) species is a key albeit poorly understood step in a number of important catalytic cycles. Herein we show through a detailed mechanistic study that this process can be elucidated by a combination of electrochemical techniques and statistical analysis and so allows us to gain a fundamental understanding of this key catalytic step in tandem catalysis. The Ni(I) species are unstable and give rise to very complex solution species and this has precluded investigation of this key step in catalysis.

Here, they used cyclic voltammetry to generate low-valent nickel species in situ and measured over 200 rate constants for oxidative addition across 16 different catalyst systems based on different ligands (bipyridine, phenanthroline, terpyridine and bis-pyrazolylpyridine, BPP) and 44 different aryl iodide species. The electronic and steric effects of the complexes on the reaction rates were studied using Linear Free-Energy Relationships (LFER) and multivariate linear regression (MLR) models. The rate constants demonstrated that the catalyst systems with bipyridine, phenanthroline, and terpyridine ligands react via a three-center concerted mechanism. In contrast, the BPP-supported Ni(I) species react through a halogen-atom abstraction mechanism.

Based on this understanding, the researchers developed a global heat map of the predicted oxidative addition rates for more than 2,000 commercially available aryl iodides. As a proof of concept, a Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling reaction was examined in detail as a test case. The predicted kinetic behavior is shown to influence the yields of the desired product and to direct the reaction into secondary pathways. The findings provide a valuable predictive tool for the optimization and design of nickel-based catalysts in cross-coupling reactions.
